title: Coordinated planning of European charging infrastructure and energy system for optimal V1G and V2G deployment - Nature Energy
gist: A cost-minimizing European energy-system model shows that simple unidirectional smart charging (V1G) delivers the bulk of system savings (up to €42bn/yr) from EV flexibility, while bidirectional vehicle-to-grid (V2G) adds only marginal system savings but can generate large balancing-market revenues in solar-heavy grids; uniform EU-wide charging infrastructure targets are shown to mis-size infrastructure needs on a country-by-country basis. Note: the retrieved text is the abstract/reference list of a paywalled article, not the full body.

themes.theme: V1G as the dominant value driver

insights.claim: In a cost-minimizing pan-European energy system model, unidirectional smart charging (V1G) captures the majority of achievable system cost savings from EV charging flexibility, estimated at €19–42 billion per year (2.2–4.5% of system cost), and substantially reduces required charging infrastructure build-out.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Grid operators and EV charging infrastructure investors should prioritize V1G (unidirectional smart charging) rollout over bidirectional V2G hardware, since V1G captures most of the system-level economic value at lower infrastructure cost.
insights.quote: V1G captures the majority of system cost savings (€19–42 billion yr−1; 2.2–4.5%) and substantially reduces infrastructure requirements

insights.claim: Vehicle-to-grid (V2G) technology provides only marginal additional system cost savings of up to €2.5 billion per year on top of V1G, despite requiring more expensive bidirectional charging hardware.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Suggests bidirectional charger manufacturers face a weak system-cost-savings case as the primary sales pitch; V2G's value proposition must instead rest on revenue capture, not systemic savings.
insights.quote: V2G provides marginal savings of up to €2.5 billion yr−1 but generates substantial balancing market revenues (€6.4 billion yr−1)

themes.theme: V2G's real business case is market revenue, not system savings

insights.claim: Although V2G adds little to total system cost savings, it can generate an estimated €6.4 billion per year in balancing market revenues across Europe, implying the commercial case for V2G rests on ancillary/balancing market participation rather than system-wide efficiency gains.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Points investors toward V2G aggregators and balancing-market revenue models (rather than utility capex avoidance) as the monetizable opportunity in bidirectional charging.

insights.claim: V2G deployment is most cost-effective in photovoltaic-dominated power systems and in scenarios with limited grid/transmission expansion, where combined solar-plus-wind output is scarce and V2G's flexibility has the highest marginal value.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Regions with high solar penetration but constrained grid buildout (e.g., southern Europe or grid-congested markets) are the most attractive early markets for V2G deployment.
insights.quote: V2G deployment is most cost-effective in photovoltaic-dominated systems and limited-grid-expansion scenarios where combined solar and wind production is scarce

themes.theme: Uniform EU charging targets are miscalibrated

insights.claim: Charging infrastructure requirements vary substantially across European countries depending on whether local flexibility strategies aim to maximize utilization or maximize smart-charging flexibility, meaning uniform EU-wide charging infrastructure targets risk overestimating needs in some countries while limiting smart-charging benefits in others.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: EU policymakers and infrastructure fund allocators should move from flat per-capita/per-vehicle charging targets to country-specific, model-informed targets to avoid stranded or under-built charging assets.
insights.quote: uniform European Union targets risk overestimating infrastructure needs in some regions while limiting the benefits of smart charging in others
